sentences.text: They tore clothes apart.
They ripped open seams.
I remember they were looking for something.
I don't know what.
Maybe money, maybe jewelery.
Anyway, things progressed to the worst.
We--I remember at that point, it wasn't very long after we came back.
But things-- the situation got so bad that my mother had nothing to give us but frozen potatoes.
At that point, my mother must of either somehow contacted my grandmother-- because you see, the ghetto was already on in Przemysl.
When I say ghetto, it wasn't closed.
You had to wear armbands already.
A grownup had to wear an armband to identify the Jews.
And Jewish-- at a certain age, you didn't have to wear it.
I didn't.
But grownups were.
And I-- I remember that.
And the people who lived all over Przemysl.
I think they were evacuated to a certain part.
I think they were squeezed in, I think, at the start-- at the beginning of the ghettos.
And I didn't know that part, because I knew some parts of Przemysl, but not that particular part.
And I remember one day, my mother said to me that I am going to my grandmother, to-- back to Grodzisko.
And that a man is going to pick me up, and he's coming and take me in the train.
And Jewish people were not allowed to go on the train no more.
They were forbidden to go on the train.
And I was scared that-- but I didn't know, really-- exactly fully understand.
But now I understand what-- how this was a dangerous thing.
But--
Just--first me, first me.
Because I was the one that knew my grandparents from Grodzisko better than my other sister.
First, I was sent for that reason because I knew my grandparents so well.
I grew up with them.
Part of the time.
Whenever I felt like it, I would go there.
And I knew-- I loved my grandmother more than-- next to my mother, there was nobody I loved more than my grandmother.
She was so close to me.
And so one day, the man took me, and we went on the train.
No.
He was a goy.
I told you, Jews were not allowed to go on a train.
Very little.
I don't know.
I don't remember what he said.
There was-- I'm sure it was very quiet.
There was very little conversation, because there wasn't supposed to be.
So that, you know-- it was supposed to be like a hush-hush thing.
And the whole thing was that she shouldn't be discovered.
You know, that he's taking a Jewish child out of where he wasn't supposed to.
So-- so he took me.
I guess-- I don't remember too much of the train.
I remember I went on a train.
The train from Przemysl to Grodzisko, which was actually [INAUDIBLE].
The station, we came to [NON-ENGLISH], which is the station of Grodzisko.
That's where we got off.
And there, he must have lived in that area.
But I don't know if I slept maybe one night-- probably, that one night, I slept in his house.
I don't remember much from it.
I was-- it's-- it's blacked out in my mind.
But I do remember in the morning, when we started to walk.
You see, in order to walk from his house to bring me to my grandmother, usually people go on the main road, but we didn't.
I remember we walked over fields to make a shortcut.
He knew how to hit straight-- a shortcut, the shortest way.
And we shouldn't be discovered.

We should-- just like a native goes and knows-- knows-- you know-- jump through a [NON-ENGLISH], a shortcut, because he knew, you know, anybody that lived-- they knew which direction you go to make a shortcut.
It was seven kilometers, this shortcut.
But he made it so it was less, so that through the fields, he headed in such a way, you know, that it was-- that it was the shortest way to get to my grandmother.
How to hit that part of where the Jewish people lived.
And that's-- I remember walking.
It was the ground was cold, frozen.
That's how I know it must have been the fall.
I don't remember the months or the day.
But I remember under my feet, I remember there was cold and frozen, like sort of the earth was not soft, like, you know, like in the summer.
I remember it was hard.
And there was like chill in the air.
You know, so I remember it was the fall.
Yes, I know it was '41.
That I know.
That was the end of-- must've been '41.
That I'm almost sure.
The end of '41.
And-- yeah, because the next spring, we still lived with my grandmother and things progressed.
Oh, it was something.
You-- I can't describe it.
She was like in heaven to receive us.
She had plenty of food.
She could cook and things.
She had what to eat.
And she cooked normal meals, and she asked me every day, no Frieda, do you love it?
That was her exact words.
Do you like it that way?
She only wanted to please us.
And we ate bread and butter.
And I remember, I didn't have bread and butter for such a long time.
And she said, that no matter how much I saw, I couldn't get enough.
My eyes, she said to me-- your eyes want more than you can eat.
I was so hungered out.
We didn't see butter for a long time-- and bread.
The bread she made.
No.
I'm coming to that, I'm coming.
After a few months, my oldest sister came.
And so we were united once more.
My older sister and me, my beloved older sister-- I loved her so much.
And I felt so good being-- having my grandmother, my sister.
And I didn't know that I will never see my mother again.
I thought this was just a temporary thing.
Nobody knew.
And we lived temporarily with my grandma.
She loved-- adored us.
She was-- we were the light of her life, because she knew that through us-- was her-- we're going to continue her-- you know, the-- in other words, we're going to live.
Continue after her--
Her line.
Her line.
Because she didn't have other children.
Her son was in America.
And she knew that there will be no-- there will be no other grandchildren.
And so she adored us to the point-- she worshipped us.
She gave us everything she could possibly to how to please us every day.
She asked us, do you like this?
Do you like the way I make it?
And every day she would ask.
And I said, yes.
Bubbe, yes.
We loved everything.
And things were like that.
And then, there were still some Jewish people.
I walked out, and this.
But to bring-- go back, I remember one day I wanted to play in the backyard.
And this goy, this goyish boy saw me.
And he threw a stone in my head.
I still have a-- just because I was a Jewish child.
He couldn't stand it.
I still have the mark in my head today, and when I feel it.
And I was afraid I will-- something will happen serious.
He threw a stone into my head, just because he didn't like a Jewish child.
So this is the kind of relationship.
This was-- But it got worse because the Germans showed such-- hated the Jews.
They took-- they took liberties with the Jewish people, too.
The-- if the Germans showed terrible atrocities, they-- they showed it too.
Before the war, this wasn't like this.
You wouldn't have thrown a stone.
Maybe have said something derogatory, but you wouldn't-- but now, it got worse.
So I was afraid to go out-- even outside of my grandmother's house.
Even though Jewish people lived-- but they were the masters now, the non-Jew.
And even though my family lived there for a generation, he felt it was his right to throw a stone at me.
I had no right there.
Even though my family, I know, lived there for a generation, in that village.
OK?
OK.
So my grandmother tried the best.
One day, she says, you know, I didn't receive a letter from your mother in a very long time, you see?
And I didn't understand what it means.
It meant, meanwhile-- now I know-- that the final solution started.
And they took them away.
They liquidated the ghettos.
And they were never seen again.
And I didn't know what happened.
I had no conception what happened.
Nobody did.
Nobody knew till they went to their death that they-- where they were going.
And they were liquidated.
They were never heard again-- not my mother, not my little sister, not her parents, my other grand-- set of grandparent-- not the uncles, and not the aunts ever came back.
Except two uncles, they managed to survive.
They were sent maybe to a different-- not to Belzec.
I was told later that all the people were taken to Belzec.
Except these two uncles must have gone to concentration camps and they survived.
They managed to survive.
By barely-- barely.
The other sisters and brothers didn't make it.
The little cousin-- who, my only little cousin who was born in Europe, because the other aunts were never married yet.
And the uncles never-- their offspring will never-- in other words, they leave nothing on this Earth.
They went to death without ever leaving anybody after them.
And that little-- little cousin, he was killed.
Moishe.
He was only three years old-- my aunt's-- my aunt's little boy.
He was born before the war, and he was killed with my aunt, and all the aunts and the uncles.
And I never saw them again.
Meanwhile, my grandmother tried to-- to carry on life as-- as much as she could for us.
She was in a familiar surrounding.
Well, she was married to that village.
And not a-- all her life, she lived there.
And she was born the next town, [NON-ENGLISH], so she knew the area way-- going for-- from one end to the other.
She knew her surroundings.
She was-- she lived-- from the First World War until the Second World, she lived in that familiar.
She knew all the way to go in every direction.
In fact, she dealt with non-Jews.
Because the non-Jewish people used to come to [NON-ENGLISH], they were the ones.
And she dealt-- she was doing, so to speak, you know, everyday business with the non-Jewish people.
But when it came to the end, somehow she couldn't find anybody to hide her.
That's what it was like.
Some people, very few, did find.
But she did not.
It's surprising.
I can't understand it today, how she didn't find somebody to hide her.
But that's how it was.
One day, I went, again, to the center.
And I have nothing to do.
I didn't go to school yet, because the war was going on.
I couldn't.
So I was amusing myself.
I went here.
I walked there.
She must have-- I didn't always follow her.
I don't know where she was.
I maybe-- I don't even remember.
You know, certain things-- things I cannot remember, I cannot tell you.
But I can only tell you what I remember.
One day, I'm going to the center.
And there was this-- this was the place where children-- like I would go there like for fun, because there was no movies.
There was no nothing to play.
But there was like a-- an ice cream store.
And I used to love to just sit there sometimes.
You know-- I don't know, that day, I don't know if I bought an ice cream.
You could sit down anyway.
And I just went into the store.
And I sit down.
And all of a sudden, I hear people talking.
And I heard them say, somebody said-- like from a grownup-- it must have been a grownup.
And they were saying that, they're coming-- the Germans are going to-- it was going to be so bad that the Germans are going to-- to hunt the Jewish people with dogs.
It was going to be so bad.
It was a preview what was coming.
And they are-- how they knew this, I don't know.
And I heard this.
And when I heard this, at that moment, I didn't say nothing.
I didn't ask nothing.
I walked home.
And from-- and as I'm sitting there in that-- it was called [POLISH] in Polish.
And this is where you got your delicious ice cream, if there was in the-- one place where you got your ice cream.
It was a fun place for a child.
And I loved to visit.
But from there on, this-- this was the warning I got.
And I made up my mind, hearing-- hearing these words, that at all odds, I'm going to cheat the Germans with their dogs.
And I am going to somehow find a way to survive.
It was from that moment-- that was the first spark in my head.
That I made up my mind-- I'm going to survive this.
And I was like a child, I was thinking.
But I was a very strong child.
I had a very special kind of will, I guess.
Because I made up my mind at that moment that there is no way they are going to kill me.
It could have been foolish, but that's how it was.
And I said, I'm going to somehow outwit the dog and the German.
And I made up my mind, I'm going to live.
I didn't-- I can't explain to you.
But I remember, that was the first time that I thought, I'm not going to let myself be killed.
And sure enough, it was like that.
Pretty soon-- one day, my grandmother said, we're going-- you see, they told other people to get on a wagon.
It was the final solution.
They finally came to us.
They finally got to our villages.
First, they went to the big cities, and they-- they, you know, took away the people, and they sent them away.
And they did whatever they wanted-- killed them.
Then they got to the smaller places, and like our village.
And that's-- that's when it started.
So my grandmother one day says, we are going into hiding.
And she says to us, we are not-- I'm not leaving-- giving you up.
She said, I'm not giving my two grandchildren up.
She says, I think that God gave me a special mission-- the fact that you were sent to me, this means that there was a purpose in it.
She was a very deep-thinking woman.
And she said, there was a purpose that you were sent to us-- to me.
And I am not giving you up.
They're going to have to come and find me.
She took me by one hand, and my sister by the other hand.
We walked out of our-- where she was living.
It was cramped quarters, but we still had a bed where to sleep.
But she walked out of there.
This was the last normal place that I lived till the war ended.
We walked out into nowhere-- into no place.
We walked out into the fields, and she told us, we're going to have to hide.
And she-- she gave us-- every night we slept.
She told us, you can go into this, sometimes this barn, because I know the man.
But he doesn't want to hide.
And we slept every night in a different barn.
And I think from this thing, from this example, I got the idea that I will-- how I will survive.
She gave-- she showed me, I think, the best way she could, that this is what-- maybe what I have to do.
At all odds.
I think that's where I got my idea.
And I was only nine and a half then.
Nine and a half, and my sister was 10 and a half.
And we slept sometimes in this barn, sometimes in that barn.
These men, I guess, people she still knew.
But they didn't want to give her a permanent hiding place.
We slept--
Three.
All three of us.
I told you, just my sister, me.
She took us by the hand, and that's how we walked out.
And we had little bundles, maybe on the back, or something.
A small bundle, not much.
I don't remember.
But we had something with us, but not much.
I don't remember.
We scrounged.
In the daytime, we would walk into the fields.
And sometimes, she would pick up a potato, and she would-- we'd eat it raw, or something.
And there were beets, or you know, like all kinds of things growing in the fields.
And she knew her surroundings.
She-- you know, this was the edge, like the end of the village.
It wasn't belonging anymore to Grodzisko.
It was like the edge, the end of this-- excuse me-- the suburbs of Grodzisko.
The surrounding areas.
There were fields.
You see, Grodzisko was surrounded in many places by woods, deep woods.
If you want-- if you went a certain direction, like towards the-- the west, there would-- I mean, woods going for miles and miles.
You-- so that there were many woods surrounding Grodzisko.
And also, fields, and you know.
And so we went in the direction, like fields and woods.
And we would-- mostly in fields.
We would sit and crouch.
And that was dangerous, because the fields belonged to people.
And if they saw us, they could have taken us just like going to somebody's home, and you stay in this house-- your enter this home.
They have a right to arrest you or to give you away.
So it was very dangerous.
But during the day, we stayed out.
And sometimes-- every day, I remember, we went a-- one time, we were staying in the woods, crouching in the woods.
And that was very scary, because at that particular time, we thought that somebody's coming to get us.
And that's the end of us.
So my grandmother went and said Viduy with us.
You know what Viduy is?
You know something?
If my grandchild would ask me to find Viduy, I don't know.
I would have to look-- search.
I wouldn't know it by heart.
But my grandmother said Viduy with us.
And she thought this was the end.
And I remember saying Viduy.
Imagine-- I'm, by right, I-- I thought I'm going to die that night-- that time.
It was towards evening, I remember.
It was-- the sun was setting.
And in fact, I remember that I had some kind of infection under my nail, and I was crying.
And my grandmother-- you know, I needed a doctor, actually.
But there was no doctor available.
And I was crying, it was hurting me.
And my grandmother said, [NON-ENGLISH].
It will heal.
And she calmed me.
And she-- and she was very calming.
Her presence was very calming to me.
But I was hurting me.
I was a little girl.
And, you know, and I remember she said-- eventually, the nail fell off, OK, but I survived.
But that day, we did survive.
We survived.
Even though I said Viduy.
And this went going on-- I remember, it wasn't very long.
I don't remember whether it was a few months or something like this.
But we never survived like this, even a year or even half a year.
Within that year, we were crouching and hiding and in fields, and everywhere you can imagine.
And it was-- it was very bad.
No, not at that-- no.
Because my grandmother was-- it was very bad.
Maybe the people who she went into the barn, maybe they gave us some food in the evening, maybe.
Because we couldn't have survived a few months without eating.
Nobody can.
But there was some-- like a potato she would get it from a field.
You know, or some things like grow-- like all kinds of things.
Like poppy seeds, you know, grew, and then the, you know, and-- Anyway, so we had something from the fields.
And at night, maybe the people that she-- you know, they allowed her to use the barn, maybe he gave her food.
I don't remember that part.
I cannot tell you something I don't remember.
I can only remember what I remember clearly, distinctly.
And it wasn't good.
But as we were crouching, and as we were sort of hiding, she gave us pep talk.
She gave us-- we asked, I remember one day, I said Bubbe-- Bubbe-- I used to call her Bubehai-- that's how I called her, Bub-- Bub-- I said, what are we going to do if we don't have food?
And she said to us, I don't think I'm going to survive.
That's what she said to us.
I don't think I'm going to make it.
But you, children, she says, you will probably make it.
I believe you will make it.
That's what she said to us in Jewish.
And I heard these words.
And I took them very seriously.
Every word she said, I listened.
I never-- in other words, before I was not so listening to the conversation.
But when she was talking to me, every word went into my-- to the deepest part of my-- of my brain, or whatever, you're supposed to take it in.
And every word was a command, when she said.
This was a command, this was not just talking.
It was like a command that you receive.
And you take it-- that's what I-- And I listened to this Bubbe, and she said, [CRYING] I'm not going to make it, but you will.
And she said like this.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She said, in [NON-ENGLISH] of your father, because he was so learned.
I hope God will grant me the wish that you will survive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And then I said to her, what are we going to eat if we don't have food and you're not around?
Because she said she doesn't think she's going to make it.
And she said to us, you're going to have to beg.
And you're going to have to survive any way you can.
You're going to beg for food if you don't have.
And that's what I did.
She said it, and I did it-- just like she said it.
And I-- and I followed her example.
I went wherever-- wherever I could later, I slept.
Or whatever I could, I did-- when it all means.
But one thing was uppermost in my mind, that I'm going to have to survive this terrible thing.
[CRYING] One day, we were sitting in the corn-- in the-- what you-- what you call corn on the cob.
It was very high.
I remember it was still summer, when the corn is about to be-- to be-- you know, harvested.
It was very high and the corn was full.
You could sit in between the rows.
And we were crouching.
And a man came.
And it must have been his field.
And he said, why we doing?
We doing in this part?
And he-- and I thought that we're going to be killed then.
But he let us alone.
He must have explained-- he must have been, I don't know, half decent, because he didn't gave us in.
And this happened once.
But another time it happened.
And this time, this goy discovered us in his-- in his-- what belonged to him.
And he was trying to-- I don't know.
I couldn't follow the conversation clearly.
But I knew enough that we are in big trouble.
And he was either trying to report us.
And she begged for her life, and her grandchildren.
While this was going on, the first time in my life I realized that I have to take command of my life.
I realized I knew enough that we are in terrible trouble.
And I knew my grandmother was preparing us that we must survive.
And I-- she didn't tell us when we have to do this, she didn't tell us now.
Like you say, now.
I didn't get no warning.
But I thought, this is it.
And I realized, this is it.
And what I did-- I looked up in the sky first, when I realized there's no hope for us.
I thought there was no hope for us.
I looked up in the sky and I asked God, are you going to let me die now, at nine and a half?
And I thought that-- that it works like this when you beg, HaShem, very much.
His presence will show himself through the sky to me.
I expected him to show himself to me, or some sign that what I must do.
I looked straight in the sky and I expected an answer.
But after a while, I realized there was no answer.
And then, it was right after this thought, that something made me-- like, as if something-- like I received, sort of like a telegram into my brain.
And the-- the telegram said run.
It was just like that.
It was right after this looking up, and I realized that it was-- I think it was a personal miracle.
Because I received this sign in my head.
It was like a thought.
It said run, run away for your life.
It was just like a telegram, believe me.
And the telegram in my head said, just run away.
Save your life.
And that's what-- and as soon as I received this thought, I didn't run, but I backed away slowly.
I wanted to see if he's going to run after me and try to prevent me.
I backed away, step by step.
Away from my grandmother.
I didn't look at my sister, if she's going to go before me.
She going to follow?
She did not.
She was standing.
And that's the way I remember the last time.
My grandmother with my sister, with my sister was standing next to her on one side.
I was standing on the other side.
And I backed back-- back.
And when I realized that he's not following me, I ran as fast as I could.
And where I was going to go, I had no idea.
I didn't know.
I didn't know who is going to hide me, or who's going to give me, or who's going to protect me.
Till that day, all my life, I had somebody to watch over me.
First, my parents, my mother.
Then, my grandmother always watched for me, and always took care of me.
But it was the first time in my life that I realized-- I didn't even realize it.
I just did the first step towards that.
And I took the step.
I walked away.
And I ran.
And I didn't-- like a wild, little animal.
I was so frightened.
If I didn't go completely out of my head that time, it's a miracle.
I should have been-- now, that-- from that night was the most scary night of my life, because it got dark soon.
And I didn't know, really, where I was going.
I only remembered here and there.
My grandmother slept in different barns, and I didn't know where to-- even one of the place, I didn't even know how to-- really, how to get there.
But what I did was, I ran away.
And when I started running, first I ran towards the direction to-- there was a house not far.
And she came out and she said, why are you running?
What's happening?
And then, I didn't answer.
I was afraid to answer.
Later, I came back to that house, but I couldn't answer.
I didn't know, I was so frightened.
But then I went to a different-- I went to the opposite direction.
And I went towards the direction where that little brook ran.
Because that brook was my guide how to-- to get my bearings.
I knew by that brook, that that brook leads to where I'm familiar.
And at first, I didn't realize I was going in the direction of where I could be identified because that was towards closer to where people lived.
And I-- and those were my enemies.
They-- they were my potential enemies.
Any person that saw me could-- knew that I-- Anyway, I went with that brook.
I ran to the closest barn where my grandmother once slept.
We slept one night.
So I knew that that man-- that non-Jew-- that man owned that barn, that he was not an unfriendly person.
Because she-- by showing me that she slept in that barn, I went there that night.
That first night, I let-- that-- the end-- when I recognized the barn.
And I let myself in.
I opened up the latch and I let myself in.
And I slept on the straw.
